input.content:
# Session Persistence Design ## Problem When OpenACP restarts, all session-to-topic mappings are lost. Telegram topics remain but OpenACP cannot reconnect sessions to them. Users must create new sessions even though `claude-agent-acp` stores conversation history on disk and supports session resume. ## Solution Lazy resume with JSON file-based session store. - Persist session metadata (including platform-specific data like `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - On restart, do NOT auto-resume any sessions (avoids spawning 100 subprocesses at once) - When user sends a message to an existing topic, lazy-resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`, reconnect to topic - If transcript is missing on disk, spawn a fresh session and update the record - Auto-cleanup records older than 30 days (configurable) ## Data Model ### SessionRecord ```typescript interface SessionRecord

> { sessionId: string // OpenACP session ID agentSessionId: string // ID from claude-agent-acp (used for resume) agentName: string workingDir: string channelId: string // "telegram" | "discord" | "slack" status: "active" | "finished" | "error" createdAt: string // ISO timestamp lastActiveAt: string // ISO timestamp, updated on each message name?: string // Auto-generated session name platform: P // Platform-specific data } ``` ### Platform Metadata Each adapter defines its own platform type: ```typescript // Telegram interface TelegramPlatformData { topicId: number } // Future: Discord interface DiscordPlatformData { threadId: string } // Future: Slack interface SlackPlatformData { threadTs: string } ``` ## SessionStore Interface ```typescript interface SessionStore { save(record: SessionRecord): void get(sessionId: string): SessionRecord | undefined findByPlatform(channelId: string, key: string, value: unknown): SessionRecord | undefined list(channelId?: string): SessionRecord[] remove(sessionId: string): void } ``` ### JsonFileSessionStore - Storage: `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - In-memory `Map` is source of truth - Debounced disk write (2s after last change) - Force flush on SIGTERM/SIGINT - Loads file once on startup - Auto-cleanup: remove records where `lastActiveAt` > TTL (default 30 days) on startup ## Lazy Resume Flow ``` User message in topic → adapter receives message with topicId → store.findByPlatform("telegram", "topicId", topicId) → Record found? → Yes: session active in memory? → Yes: forward message normally → No: spawn agent → resume(agentSessionId) → attach to topic → forward → No: spawn new session → create new record with topicId → forward ``` - User experience is seamless — send message, get reply - First message after restart adds ~2-3s latency (spawn + ACP handshake + resume) - If resume fails (transcript deleted) → spawn fresh session, update record ## Record Lifecycle ### Writes - `save()` on new session creation or successful resume - Update `lastActiveAt` on each message (debounced 2s) - Update `status` when session finishes or errors ### Auto-cleanup - Runs once on startup - Removes records where `lastActiveAt` > `sessionStore.ttlDays` (default 30) - Only removes records, does NOT delete Telegram topics - TODO: Notify user about expired topics (not implemented) ### Debounce - In-memory Map is source of truth - Flush to disk 2s after last change - Force flush on process shutdown ## Integration Points ### New File - `src/core/session-store.ts` — `SessionStore` interface + `JsonFileSessionStore` ### Core Changes - **SessionManager** — inject `SessionStore`, call `save()`/`remove()` on create/destroy - **Session** — add `agentSessionId` field (from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn) ### AgentInstance Changes - Add static `resume()` method — like `spawn()` but calls `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)` instead of `newSession()` ### Telegram Adapter Changes - Message handler: when receiving message for topic without active session → trigger lazy resume - Platform metadata type: `{ topicId: number }` ### Config Changes - Add `sessionStore.ttlDays` to config schema (default 30) ## Design Decisions 1. **Lazy over eager resume** — Avoids spawning N subprocesses on startup. Only resume when user actually sends a message. 2. **JSON file over SQLite** — Simpler, no extra dependency. SessionStore interface allows swapping to SQLite later if needed. 3. **Core-level store, adapter-level platform data** — Adapters share storage logic via `SessionStore`, only define their own `platform` fields. Adding a new adapter = define platform type + use existing store API. 4. **Auto-cleanup records, not topics** — Records are internal data, safe to auto-delete. Topics are user-visible, should not be auto-deleted.

toolUseResult.content:
# Session Persistence Design ## Problem When OpenACP restarts, all session-to-topic mappings are lost. Telegram topics remain but OpenACP cannot reconnect sessions to them. Users must create new sessions even though `claude-agent-acp` stores conversation history on disk and supports session resume. ## Solution Lazy resume with JSON file-based session store. - Persist session metadata (including platform-specific data like `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - On restart, do NOT auto-resume any sessions (avoids spawning 100 subprocesses at once) - When user sends a message to an existing topic, lazy-resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`, reconnect to topic - If transcript is missing on disk, spawn a fresh session and update the record - Auto-cleanup records older than 30 days (configurable) ## Data Model ### SessionRecord ```typescript interface SessionRecord

> { sessionId: string // OpenACP session ID agentSessionId: string // ID from claude-agent-acp (used for resume) agentName: string workingDir: string channelId: string // "telegram" | "discord" | "slack" status: "active" | "finished" | "error" createdAt: string // ISO timestamp lastActiveAt: string // ISO timestamp, updated on each message name?: string // Auto-generated session name platform: P // Platform-specific data } ``` ### Platform Metadata Each adapter defines its own platform type: ```typescript // Telegram interface TelegramPlatformData { topicId: number } // Future: Discord interface DiscordPlatformData { threadId: string } // Future: Slack interface SlackPlatformData { threadTs: string } ``` ## SessionStore Interface ```typescript interface SessionStore { save(record: SessionRecord): void get(sessionId: string): SessionRecord | undefined findByPlatform(channelId: string, key: string, value: unknown): SessionRecord | undefined list(channelId?: string): SessionRecord[] remove(sessionId: string): void } ``` ### JsonFileSessionStore - Storage: `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - In-memory `Map` is source of truth - Debounced disk write (2s after last change) - Force flush on SIGTERM/SIGINT - Loads file once on startup - Auto-cleanup: remove records where `lastActiveAt` > TTL (default 30 days) on startup ## Lazy Resume Flow ``` User message in topic → adapter receives message with topicId → store.findByPlatform("telegram", "topicId", topicId) → Record found? → Yes: session active in memory? → Yes: forward message normally → No: spawn agent → resume(agentSessionId) → attach to topic → forward → No: spawn new session → create new record with topicId → forward ``` - User experience is seamless — send message, get reply - First message after restart adds ~2-3s latency (spawn + ACP handshake + resume) - If resume fails (transcript deleted) → spawn fresh session, update record ## Record Lifecycle ### Writes - `save()` on new session creation or successful resume - Update `lastActiveAt` on each message (debounced 2s) - Update `status` when session finishes or errors ### Auto-cleanup - Runs once on startup - Removes records where `lastActiveAt` > `sessionStore.ttlDays` (default 30) - Only removes records, does NOT delete Telegram topics - TODO: Notify user about expired topics (not implemented) ### Debounce - In-memory Map is source of truth - Flush to disk 2s after last change - Force flush on process shutdown ## Integration Points ### New File - `src/core/session-store.ts` — `SessionStore` interface + `JsonFileSessionStore` ### Core Changes - **SessionManager** — inject `SessionStore`, call `save()`/`remove()` on create/destroy - **Session** — add `agentSessionId` field (from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn) ### AgentInstance Changes - Add static `resume()` method — like `spawn()` but calls `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)` instead of `newSession()` ### Telegram Adapter Changes - Message handler: when receiving message for topic without active session → trigger lazy resume - Platform metadata type: `{ topicId: number }` ### Config Changes - Add `sessionStore.ttlDays` to config schema (default 30) ## Design Decisions 1. **Lazy over eager resume** — Avoids spawning N subprocesses on startup. Only resume when user actually sends a message. 2. **JSON file over SQLite** — Simpler, no extra dependency. SessionStore interface allows swapping to SQLite later if needed. 3. **Core-level store, adapter-level platform data** — Adapters share storage logic via `SessionStore`, only define their own `platform` fields. Adding a new adapter = define platform type + use existing store API. 4. **Auto-cleanup records, not topics** — Records are internal data, safe to auto-delete. Topics are user-visible, should not be auto-deleted.

content.text:
Now I have a thorough understanding of both the design spec and the existing codebase. Let me compile my review.

---

## Design Spec Review: Session Persistence

### Overall Assessment

The spec is well-structured, addresses a real problem, and the lazy resume approach is the right call. However, I found several issues that need attention before implementation.

---

### CRITICAL Issues

**1. `unstable_resumeSession` exists but the spec references the wrong API shape**

The spec says the `AgentInstance` should add a static `resume()` method that "calls `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`". However, looking at the actual ACP SDK (`@agentclientprotocol/sdk` v0.16.1), `ResumeSessionRequest` requires `{ sessionId, cwd, mcpServers? }` -- it takes a `cwd` parameter, not just a session ID. The spec's `AgentInstance.resume()` description does not account for passing `cwd`. The resume flow must also re-run the full ACP handshake (`initialize()`) before calling `unstable_resumeSession()`, since a new subprocess is spawned each time. This needs to be explicitly stated.

**2. `SessionStore.save()` returns `void` -- should be async**

The `SessionStore` interface shows `save(record: SessionRecord): void`. Since `JsonFileSessionStore` does debounced disk writes, the in-memory part is synchronous, but the interface should still be `Promise<void>` for forward compatibility (e.g., swapping to SQLite later, as the spec mentions). Same applies to `remove()`.

**3. Race condition: concurrent lazy resume for the same topic**

The lazy resume flow does not address what happens when two messages arrive rapidly for the same topic after restart. Both would see "no active session in memory" and both would try to spawn an agent and resume. The spec needs a "resuming" lock/sentinel per topic to prevent duplicate spawns.

---

### IMPORTANT Issues

**4. `SessionRecord.status` does not match existing `SessionStatus` type**

The existing codebase defines `SessionStatus = 'initializing' | 'active' | 'cancelled' | 'finished' | 'error'`. The spec's `SessionRecord.status` only includes `"active" | "finished" | "error"`. Missing `"initializing"` and `"cancelled"`. At minimum, add `"cancelled"` since a cancelled session should not be resumable. Consider also adding `"initializing"` or just reusing the existing `SessionStatus` type for consistency.

**5. `findByPlatform` signature uses `unknown` for value -- too loose**

`findByPlatform(channelId: string, key: string, value: unknown)` is stringly-typed and type-unsafe. Since `platform` is generic `P`, consider a type-safe lookup:

```typescript
findByPlatformField<K extends string>(
  channelId: string, 
  key: K, 
  value: unknown
): SessionRecord | undefined
```

Or better yet, accept a predicate: `findByPlatform(channelId: string, predicate: (platform: Record<string, unknown>) => boolean)`.

**6. No handling of `agentSessionId` population timing**

The spec says `Session` should add an `agentSessionId` field from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn. But `AgentInstance.sessionId` is set inside `static spawn()` (line 108 of agent-instance.js: `instance.sessionId = response.sessionId`). The spec should clarify exactly when `SessionRecord` is first saved -- it must be AFTER `AgentInstance.spawn()` completes so `agentSessionId` is available. Currently, `SessionManager.createSession()` calls `agentManager.spawn()` first, then creates the `Session`, so this works, but the spec should explicitly note this ordering requirement.

**7. No mention of `listSessions` from the ACP SDK**

The ACP SDK v0.16.1 provides `connection.listSessions()` which returns sessions with metadata. Before spawning a new agent and calling `unstable_resumeSession`, the system could first call `listSessions` to verify the session still exists on the agent side. This would be more robust than catching resume failures after the fact.

**8. Spec says "new file `src/core/session-store.ts`" but source is not in `src/`**

The existing project has compiled output in `packages/core/dist/`. The spec references `src/core/session-store.ts` but based on the monorepo layout in CLAUDE.md, the actual source path should be something like `packages/core/src/session-store.ts` (or wherever the `.ts` source files are located). This path needs correction.

---

### SUGGESTIONS (Nice to Have)

**9. Consider `loadSession` as an alternative to `unstable_resumeSession`**

The ACP SDK exposes both `loadSession` (stable, replays history) and `unstable_resumeSession` (experimental, no history replay). Since OpenACP does not need to replay the history to the user (Telegram topics already have it), `unstable_resumeSession` is correct. However, the spec should note the risk: this API is marked `@experimental` and "may be removed or changed at any point". Consider having a fallback to `loadSession` if `unstable_resumeSession` is not supported by the agent.

**10. Auto-cleanup only on startup may leave stale records for long-running instances**

If OpenACP runs for months without restart, records older than 30 days will accumulate. Consider adding a periodic cleanup (e.g., daily interval timer) in addition to startup cleanup.

**11. Missing consideration: what happens to `pendingPermission` on resume?**

If a session was killed while waiting for a permission response, the agent side might have a stale pending permission. After resume, the agent could re-request permission, but the spec does not address this edge case.

**12. `SessionRecord` uses `string` for `createdAt`/`lastActiveAt` (ISO timestamps)**

This is fine for JSON serialization, but the existing `Session` class uses `createdAt: Date`. The spec should note the conversion between `Date` (in-memory Session) and ISO string (in SessionRecord) explicitly to avoid confusion during implementation.

**13. No versioning for `sessions.json` format**

If the schema changes in a future release, there is no `version` field in the file to handle migrations. Consider adding `{ version: 1, sessions: [...] }` as the file format.

---

### What Is Done Well

- The lazy resume approach is the correct design -- avoids the thundering herd problem on restart.
- The `SessionStore` interface is properly abstracted to allow future storage backends.
- Platform-specific data is correctly separated via generics.
- Auto-cleanup with configurable TTL is sensible.
- The "do not delete Telegram topics" decision is the right call for user experience.
- Debounced writes with force-flush on shutdown is a solid persistence strategy.

---

### Verdict

**Not approved yet.** Three critical issues need resolution before implementation:

1. Complete the `resume()` method spec to include the full ACP handshake flow and `cwd` parameter
2. Make `SessionStore` methods async
3. Add a per-topic resume lock to prevent duplicate spawns from concurrent messages

The important issues (4-8) should also be addressed but could potentially be resolved during implementation if the spec is updated with TODO notes.

file.content:
# Session Persistence Design ## Problem When OpenACP restarts, all session-to-topic mappings are lost. Telegram topics remain but OpenACP cannot reconnect sessions to them. Users must create new sessions even though `claude-agent-acp` stores conversation history on disk and supports session resume. ## Solution Lazy resume with JSON file-based session store. - Persist session metadata (including platform-specific data like `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - On restart, do NOT auto-resume any sessions (avoids spawning 100 subprocesses at once) - When user sends a message to an existing topic, lazy-resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`, reconnect to topic - If transcript is missing on disk, spawn a fresh session and update the record - Auto-cleanup records older than 30 days (configurable) ## Data Model ### SessionRecord ```typescript interface SessionRecord

> { sessionId: string // OpenACP session ID agentSessionId: string // ID from claude-agent-acp (used for resume) agentName: string workingDir: string channelId: string // "telegram" | "discord" | "slack" status: "active" | "finished" | "error" createdAt: string // ISO timestamp lastActiveAt: string // ISO timestamp, updated on each message name?: string // Auto-generated session name platform: P // Platform-specific data } ``` ### Platform Metadata Each adapter defines its own platform type: ```typescript // Telegram interface TelegramPlatformData { topicId: number } // Future: Discord interface DiscordPlatformData { threadId: string } // Future: Slack interface SlackPlatformData { threadTs: string } ``` ## SessionStore Interface ```typescript interface SessionStore { save(record: SessionRecord): void get(sessionId: string): SessionRecord | undefined findByPlatform(channelId: string, key: string, value: unknown): SessionRecord | undefined list(channelId?: string): SessionRecord[] remove(sessionId: string): void } ``` ### JsonFileSessionStore - Storage: `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - In-memory `Map` is source of truth - Debounced disk write (2s after last change) - Force flush on SIGTERM/SIGINT - Loads file once on startup - Auto-cleanup: remove records where `lastActiveAt` > TTL (default 30 days) on startup ## Lazy Resume Flow ``` User message in topic → adapter receives message with topicId → store.findByPlatform("telegram", "topicId", topicId) → Record found? → Yes: session active in memory? → Yes: forward message normally → No: spawn agent → resume(agentSessionId) → attach to topic → forward → No: spawn new session → create new record with topicId → forward ``` - User experience is seamless — send message, get reply - First message after restart adds ~2-3s latency (spawn + ACP handshake + resume) - If resume fails (transcript deleted) → spawn fresh session, update record ## Record Lifecycle ### Writes - `save()` on new session creation or successful resume - Update `lastActiveAt` on each message (debounced 2s) - Update `status` when session finishes or errors ### Auto-cleanup - Runs once on startup - Removes records where `lastActiveAt` > `sessionStore.ttlDays` (default 30) - Only removes records, does NOT delete Telegram topics - TODO: Notify user about expired topics (not implemented) ### Debounce - In-memory Map is source of truth - Flush to disk 2s after last change - Force flush on process shutdown ## Integration Points ### New File - `src/core/session-store.ts` — `SessionStore` interface + `JsonFileSessionStore` ### Core Changes - **SessionManager** — inject `SessionStore`, call `save()`/`remove()` on create/destroy - **Session** — add `agentSessionId` field (from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn) ### AgentInstance Changes - Add static `resume()` method — like `spawn()` but calls `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)` instead of `newSession()` ### Telegram Adapter Changes - Message handler: when receiving message for topic without active session → trigger lazy resume - Platform metadata type: `{ topicId: number }` ### Config Changes - Add `sessionStore.ttlDays` to config schema (default 30) ## Design Decisions 1. **Lazy over eager resume** — Avoids spawning N subprocesses on startup. Only resume when user actually sends a message. 2. **JSON file over SQLite** — Simpler, no extra dependency. SessionStore interface allows swapping to SQLite later if needed. 3. **Core-level store, adapter-level platform data** — Adapters share storage logic via `SessionStore`, only define their own `platform` fields. Adding a new adapter = define platform type + use existing store API. 4. **Auto-cleanup records, not topics** — Records are internal data, safe to auto-delete. Topics are user-visible, should not be auto-deleted.

# Session Persistence Design ## Problem When OpenACP restarts, all session-to-topic mappings are lost. Telegram topics remain but OpenACP cannot reconnect sessions to them. Users must create new sessions even though `claude-agent-acp` stores conversation history on disk and supports session resume. ## Solution Lazy resume with JSON file-based session store. - Persist session metadata (including platform-specific data like `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - On restart, do NOT auto-resume any sessions (avoids spawning 100 subprocesses at once) - When user sends a message to an existing topic, lazy-resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`, reconnect to topic - If transcript is missing on disk, spawn a fresh session and update the record - Auto-cleanup records older than 30 days (configurable) ## Data Model ### SessionRecord ```typescript interface SessionRecord

> { sessionId: string // OpenACP session ID agentSessionId: string // ID from claude-agent-acp (used for resume) agentName: string workingDir: string channelId: string // "telegram" | "discord" | "slack" status: SessionStatus // reuse existing type: "initializing" | "active" | "cancelled" | "finished" | "error" createdAt: string // ISO timestamp (converted from Date on save) lastActiveAt: string // ISO timestamp, updated on each message name?: string // Auto-generated session name platform: P // Platform-specific data } ``` ### Platform Metadata Each adapter defines its own platform type: ```typescript // Telegram interface TelegramPlatformData { topicId: number } // Future: Discord interface DiscordPlatformData { threadId: string } // Future: Slack interface SlackPlatformData { threadTs: string } ``` ### File Format ```json { "version": 1, "sessions": { ... } } ``` Version field enables future schema migrations. ## SessionStore Interface ```typescript interface SessionStore { save(record: SessionRecord): Promise get(sessionId: string): SessionRecord | undefined findByPlatform(channelId: string, predicate: (platform: Record) => boolean): SessionRecord | undefined list(channelId?: string): SessionRecord[] remove(sessionId: string): Promise } ``` Note: `save()` and `remove()` are async for forward compatibility (SQLite swap). `get()`, `findByPlatform()`, `list()` are sync since they read from in-memory Map only. `findByPlatform` uses a predicate instead of stringly-typed key/value for type safety: ```typescript store.findByPlatform("telegram", (p) => p.topicId === 123) ``` ### JsonFileSessionStore - Storage: `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - In-memory `Map` is source of truth - Debounced disk write (2s after last change) - Force flush on SIGTERM/SIGINT - Loads file once on startup - Auto-cleanup on startup: remove records where `lastActiveAt` > TTL (default 30 days) - Periodic cleanup: daily interval timer for long-running instances ## Lazy Resume Flow ``` User message in topic → adapter receives message with topicId → store.findByPlatform("telegram", p => p.topicId === topicId) → Record found? → Yes: session active in memory? → Yes: forward message normally → No: acquire resume lock for topicId (prevent concurrent resume) → spawn agent subprocess → ACP handshake: connection.initialize() → call connection.unstable_resumeSession({ sessionId: agentSessionId, cwd: workingDir }) → if resume fails → fallback to connection.newSession({ cwd }) → wire events, attach to topic → release lock, forward message → No: spawn new session → create new record with topicId → forward ``` ### Concurrency Guard A per-topic resume lock prevents duplicate spawns when multiple messages arrive for the same topic before resume completes. Messages arriving during resume are queued and forwarded after resume finishes. ### Resume Method Detail `AgentInstance.resume()` follows the same flow as `spawn()`: 1. Resolve agent command 2. Spawn subprocess with stdio pipes 3. Create ACP connection 4. **`connection.initialize()`** — full ACP handshake (required for new subprocess) 5. **`connection.unstable_resumeSession({ sessionId, cwd })`** instead of `newSession()` 6. Set up crash detection and event wiring 7. If `unstable_resumeSession` fails → fallback to `newSession({ cwd })` Note: `unstable_resumeSession` is marked `@experimental` in the ACP SDK. If removed in a future version, fallback to `loadSession()` (stable, replays history). ### Save Timing `SessionRecord` is saved AFTER `AgentInstance.spawn()` completes, since `agentSessionId` is only available after ACP session creation. ## Record Lifecycle ### Writes - `save()` on new session creation (after spawn completes and `agentSessionId` is available) - `save()` on successful resume (update `agentSessionId` if it changed) - Update `lastActiveAt` on each message (debounced 2s) - Update `status` when session finishes, cancels, or errors ### Auto-cleanup - Runs on startup + daily interval timer - Removes records where `lastActiveAt` > `sessionStore.ttlDays` (default 30) - Skips records with status `"active"` regardless of age - Only removes records, does NOT delete Telegram topics - TODO: Notify user about expired topics (not implemented) ### Debounce - In-memory Map is source of truth - Flush to disk 2s after last change - Force flush on process shutdown ## Integration Points ### New File - `src/core/session-store.ts` — `SessionStore` interface + `JsonFileSessionStore` ### Core Changes - **SessionManager** — inject `SessionStore`, call `save()`/`remove()` on create/destroy - **Session** — add `agentSessionId` field (from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn completes) ### AgentInstance Changes - Add static `resume()` method — same as `spawn()` but uses `unstable_resumeSession({ sessionId, cwd })` instead of `newSession()`, with fallback to `newSession()` on failure ### Telegram Adapter Changes - Message handler: when receiving message for topic without active session → acquire resume lock → trigger lazy resume → release lock - Queue messages arriving during resume, forward after completion - Platform metadata type: `{ topicId: number }` ### Config Changes - Add `sessionStore.ttlDays` to config schema (default 30) ## Edge Cases - **Permission pending on restart**: Agent may re-request permission after resume. Existing permission handling in adapter will handle this naturally. - **Cancelled sessions**: Records with status `"cancelled"` are not resumable. User message in a cancelled session's topic spawns a fresh session. - **Long-running instances**: Daily cleanup timer prevents stale record accumulation without requiring restart. ## Design Decisions 1. **Lazy over eager resume** — Avoids spawning N subprocesses on startup. Only resume when user actually sends a message. 2. **JSON file over SQLite** — Simpler, no extra dependency. SessionStore interface allows swapping to SQLite later if needed. Version field in file format enables future migrations. 3. **Core-level store, adapter-level platform data** — Adapters share storage logic via `SessionStore`, only define their own `platform` fields. Adding a new adapter = define platform type + use existing store API. 4. **Auto-cleanup records, not topics** — Records are internal data, safe to auto-delete. Topics are user-visible, should not be auto-deleted. 5. **Predicate-based lookup** — `findByPlatform` uses predicate for type safety instead of stringly-typed key/value. 6. **Resume lock** — Per-topic lock prevents race condition from concurrent messages during lazy resume.

Users must create new sessions even though `claude-agent-acp` stores conversation history on disk and supports session resume. ## Solution Lazy resume with JSON file-based session store. - Persist session metadata (including platform-specific data like `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - On restart, do NOT auto-resume any sessions (avoids spawning 100 subprocesses at once) - When user sends a message to an existing topic, lazy-resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`, reconnect to topic - If transcript is missing on disk, spawn a fresh session and update the record - Auto-cleanup records older than 30 days (configurable) ## Data Model ### SessionRecord ```typescript interface SessionRecord

> { sessionId: string // OpenACP session ID agentSessionId: string // ID from claude-agent-acp (used for resume) agentName: string workingDir: string channelId: string // "telegram" | "discord" | "slack" status: "active" | "finished" | "error" createdAt: string // ISO timestamp lastActiveAt: string // ISO timestamp, updated on each message name?: string // Auto-generated session name platform: P // Platform-specific data } ``` ### Platform Metadata Each adapter defines its own platform type: ```typescript // Telegram interface TelegramPlatformData { topicId: number } // Future: Discord interface DiscordPlatformData { threadId: string } // Future: Slack interface SlackPlatformData { threadTs: string } ``` ## SessionStore Interface ```typescript interface SessionStore { save(record: SessionRecord): void get(sessionId: string): SessionRecord | undefined findByPlatform(channelId: string, key: string, value: unknown): SessionRecord | undefined list(channelId?: string): SessionRecord[] remove(sessionId: string): void } ``` ### JsonFileSessionStore - Storage: `~/.openacp/sessions.json` - In-memory `Map` is source of truth - Debounced disk write (2s after last change) - Force flush on SIGTERM/SIGINT - Loads file once on startup - Auto-cleanup: remove records where `lastActiveAt` > TTL (default 30 days) on startup ## Lazy Resume Flow ``` User message in topic → adapter receives message with topicId → store.findByPlatform("telegram", "topicId", topicId) → Record found? → Yes: session active in memory? → Yes: forward message normally → No: spawn agent → resume(agentSessionId) → attach to topic → forward → No: spawn new session → create new record with topicId → forward ``` - User experience is seamless — send message, get reply - First message after restart adds ~2-3s latency (spawn + ACP handshake + resume) - If resume fails (transcript deleted) → spawn fresh session, update record ## Record Lifecycle ### Writes - `save()` on new session creation or successful resume - Update `lastActiveAt` on each message (debounced 2s) - Update `status` when session finishes or errors ### Auto-cleanup - Runs once on startup - Removes records where `lastActiveAt` > `sessionStore.ttlDays` (default 30) - Only removes records, does NOT delete Telegram topics - TODO: Notify user about expired topics (not implemented) ### Debounce - In-memory Map is source of truth - Flush to disk 2s after last change - Force flush on process shutdown ## Integration Points ### New File - `src/core/session-store.ts` — `SessionStore` interface + `JsonFileSessionStore` ### Core Changes - **SessionManager** — inject `SessionStore`, call `save()`/`remove()` on create/destroy - **Session** — add `agentSessionId` field (from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn) ### AgentInstance Changes - Add static `resume()` method — like `spawn()` but calls `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)` instead of `newSession()` ### Telegram Adapter Changes - Message handler: when receiving message for topic without active session → trigger lazy resume - Platform metadata type: `{ topicId: number }` ### Config Changes - Add `sessionStore.ttlDays` to config schema (default 30) ## Design Decisions 1. **Lazy over eager resume** — Avoids spawning N subprocesses on startup. Only resume when user actually sends a message. 2. **JSON file over SQLite** — Simpler, no extra dependency. SessionStore interface allows swapping to SQLite later if needed. 3. **Core-level store, adapter-level platform data** — Adapters share storage logic via `SessionStore`, only define their own `platform` fields. Adding a new adapter = define platform type + use existing store API. 4. **Auto-cleanup records, not topics** — Records are internal data, safe to auto-delete. content.text:
Spec đã được viết và cập nhật theo review feedback. Các điểm đã fix:

1. **Resume flow chi tiết** — bao gồm full ACP handshake + `cwd` parameter + fallback khi fail
2. **Async interface** — `save()`/`remove()` trả `Promise<void>` cho forward compatibility
3. **Resume lock** — chống race condition khi nhiều message đến cùng topic cùng lúc
4. **Status reuse** — dùng `SessionStatus` type hiện có thay vì define mới
5. **Predicate lookup** — `findByPlatform` dùng predicate thay vì stringly-typed
6. **File versioning** — thêm `version: 1` cho future migration
7. **Periodic cleanup** — thêm daily timer ngoài startup cleanup

Spec nằm tại `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-03-20-session-persistence-design.md`. Bạn review xem có muốn thay đổi gì trước khi chuyển sang viết implementation plan không?

message.content: ok chuyển sang implementation đi

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/kienduong/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.5/skills/writing-plans

# Writing Plans

## Overview

Write comprehensive implementation plans assuming the engineer has zero context for our codebase and questionable taste. Document everything they need to know: which files to touch for each task, code, testing, docs they might need to check, how to test it. Give them the whole plan as bite-sized tasks. DRY. YAGNI. TDD. Frequent commits.

Assume they are a skilled developer, but know almost nothing about our toolset or problem domain. Assume they don't know good test design very well.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the writing-plans skill to create the implementation plan."

**Context:** This should be run in a dedicated worktree (created by brainstorming skill).

**Save plans to:** `docs/superpowers/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<feature-name>.md`
- (User preferences for plan location override this default)

## Scope Check

If the spec covers multiple independent subsystems, it should have been broken into sub-project specs during brainstorming. If it wasn't, suggest breaking this into separate plans — one per subsystem. Each plan should produce working, testable software on its own.

## File Structure

Before defining tasks, map out which files will be created or modified and what each one is responsible for. This is where decomposition decisions get locked in.

- Design units with clear boundaries and well-defined interfaces. Each file should have one clear responsibility.
- You reason best about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. Prefer smaller, focused files over large ones that do too much.
- Files that change together should live together. Split by responsibility, not by technical layer.
- In existing codebases, follow established patterns. If the codebase uses large files, don't unilaterally restructure - but if a file you're modifying has grown unwieldy, including a split in the plan is reasonable.

This structure informs the task decomposition. Each task should produce self-contained changes that make sense independently.

## Bite-Sized Task Granularity

**Each step is one action (2-5 minutes):**
- "Write the failing test" - step
- "Run it to make sure it fails" - step
- "Implement the minimal code to make the test pass" - step
- "Run the tests and make sure they pass" - step
- "Commit" - step

## Plan Document Header

**Every plan MUST start with this header:**

```markdown
# [Feature Name] Implementation Plan

> **For agentic workers:** REQUIRED SUB-SKILL: Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development (recommended) or superpowers:executing-plans to implement this plan task-by-task. Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking.

**Goal:** [One sentence describing what this builds]

**Architecture:** [2-3 sentences about approach]

**Tech Stack:** [Key technologies/libraries]

---
```

## Task Structure

````markdown
### Task N: [Component Name]

**Files:**
- Create: `exact/path/to/file.py`
- Modify: `exact/path/to/existing.py:123-145`
- Test: `tests/exact/path/to/test.py`

- [ ] **Step 1: Write the failing test**

```python
def test_specific_behavior():
    result = function(input)
    assert result == expected
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Run test to verify it fails**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: FAIL with "function not defined"

- [ ] **Step 3: Write minimal implementation**

```python
def function(input):
    return expected
```

- [ ] **Step 4: Run test to verify it passes**

Run: `pytest tests/path/test.py::test_name -v`
Expected: PASS

- [ ] **Step 5: Commit**

```bash
git add tests/path/test.py src/path/file.py
git commit -m "feat: add specific feature"
```
````

## Remember
- Exact file paths always
- Complete code in plan (not "add validation")
- Exact commands with expected output
- Reference relevant skills with @ syntax
- DRY, YAGNI, TDD, frequent commits

## Plan Review Loop

After writing the complete plan:

1. Dispatch a single plan-document-reviewer subagent (see plan-document-reviewer-prompt.md) with precisely crafted review context — never your session history. This keeps the reviewer focused on the plan, not your thought process.
   - Provide: path to the plan document, path to spec document
2. If ❌ Issues Found: fix the issues, re-dispatch reviewer for the whole plan
3. If ✅ Approved: proceed to execution handoff

**Review loop guidance:**
- Same agent that wrote the plan fixes it (preserves context)
- If loop exceeds 3 iterations, surface to human for guidance
- Reviewers are advisory — explain disagreements if you believe feedback is incorrect

## Execution Handoff

After saving the plan, offer execution choice:

**"Plan complete and saved to `docs/superpowers/plans/<filename>.md`. Two execution options:**

**1. Subagent-Driven (recommended)** - I dispatch a fresh subagent per task, review between tasks, fast iteration

**2. Inline Execution** - Execute tasks in this session using executing-plans, batch execution with checkpoints

**Which approach?"**

**If Subagent-Driven chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:subagent-driven-development
- Fresh subagent per task + two-stage review

**If Inline Execution chosen:**
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:executing-plans
- Batch execution with checkpoints for review

1→import { ConfigManager } from './config.js'
     2→import { AgentManager } from './agent-manager.js'
     3→import { SessionManager } from './session-manager.js'
     4→import { NotificationManager } from './notification.js'
     5→import { ChannelAdapter } from './channel.js'
     6→import { Session } from './session.js'
     7→import type { IncomingMessage, AgentEvent, OutgoingMessage, PermissionRequest } from './types.js'
     8→import { createChildLogger } from './log.js'
     9→const log = createChildLogger({ module: 'core' })
    10→
    11→export class OpenACPCore {
    12→  configManager: ConfigManager
    13→  agentManager: AgentManager
    14→  sessionManager: SessionManager
    15→  notificationManager: NotificationManager
    16→  adapters: Map<string, ChannelAdapter> = new Map()
    17→
    18→  constructor(configManager: ConfigManager) {
    19→    this.configManager = configManager
    20→    const config = configManager.get()
    21→    this.agentManager = new AgentManager(config)
    22→    this.sessionManager = new SessionManager()
    23→    this.notificationManager = new NotificationManager(this.adapters)
    24→  }
    25→
    26→  registerAdapter(name: string, adapter: ChannelAdapter): void {
    27→    this.adapters.set(name, adapter)
    28→  }
    29→
    30→  async start(): Promise<void> {
    31→    for (const adapter of this.adapters.values()) {
    32→      await adapter.start()
    33→    }
    34→  }
    35→
    36→  async stop(): Promise<void> {
    37→    // 1. Notify users
    38→    try {
    39→      await this.notificationManager.notifyAll({
    40→        sessionId: 'system',
    41→        type: 'error',
    42→        summary: 'OpenACP is shutting down',
    43→      })
    44→    } catch { /* best effort */ }
    45→
    46→    // 2. Destroy all sessions
    47→    await this.sessionManager.destroyAll()
    48→
    49→    // 3. Stop adapters
    50→    for (const adapter of this.adapters.values()) {
    51→      await adapter.stop()
    52→    }
    53→  }
    54→
    55→  // --- Message Routing ---
    56→
    57→  async handleMessage(message: IncomingMessage): Promise<void> {
    58→    const config = this.configManager.get()
    59→    log.debug({ channelId: message.channelId, threadId: message.threadId, userId: message.userId }, 'Incoming message')
    60→
    61→    // Security: check allowed user IDs
    62→    if (config.security.allowedUserIds.length > 0) {
    63→      if (!config.security.allowedUserIds.includes(message.userId)) {
    64→        log.warn({ userId: message.userId }, 'Rejected message from unauthorized user')
    65→        return
    66→      }
    67→    }
    68→
    69→    // Check concurrent session limit
    70→    const activeSessions = this.sessionManager.listSessions()
    71→      .filter(s => s.status === 'active' || s.status === 'initializing')
    72→    if (activeSessions.length >= config.security.maxConcurrentSessions) {
    73→      log.warn({ userId: message.userId, currentCount: activeSessions.length, max: config.security.maxConcurrentSessions }, 'Session limit reached')
    74→      const adapter = this.adapters.get(message.channelId)
    75→      if (adapter) {
    76→        await adapter.sendMessage('system', {
    77→          type: 'error',
    78→          text: `Max concurrent sessions (${config.security.maxConcurrentSessions}) reached. Cancel a session first.`,
    79→        })
    80→      }
    81→      return
    82→    }
    83→
    84→    // Find session by thread
    85→    const session = this.sessionManager.getSessionByThread(message.channelId, message.threadId)
    86→    if (!session) return
    87→
    88→    // Forward to session
    89→    await session.enqueuePrompt(message.text)
    90→  }
    91→
    92→  async handleNewSession(
    93→    channelId: string,
    94→    agentName?: string,
    95→    workspacePath?: string,
    96→  ): Promise<Session> {
    97→    const config = this.configManager.get()
    98→    const resolvedAgent = agentName || config.defaultAgent
    99→    log.info({ channelId, agentName: resolvedAgent }, 'New session request')
   100→    const resolvedWorkspace = this.configManager.resolveWorkspace(
   101→      workspacePath || config.agents[resolvedAgent]?.workingDirectory
   102→    )
   103→
   104→    const session = await this.sessionManager.createSession(
   105→      channelId, resolvedAgent, resolvedWorkspace, this.agentManager
   106→    )
   107→
   108→    // Wire events
   109→    const adapter = this.adapters.get(channelId)
   110→    if (adapter) {
   111→      this.wireSessionEvents(session, adapter)
   112→    }
   113→
   114→    return session
   115→  }
   116→
   117→  async handleNewChat(
   118→    channelId: string,
   119→    currentThreadId: string,
   120→  ): Promise<Session | null> {
   121→    const currentSession = this.sessionManager.getSessionByThread(channelId, currentThreadId)
   122→    if (!currentSession) return null
   123→
   124→    return this.handleNewSession(
   125→      channelId,
   126→      currentSession.agentName,
   127→      currentSession.workingDirectory,
   128→    )
   129→  }
   130→
   131→  // --- Event Wiring ---
   132→
   133→  private toOutgoingMessage(event: AgentEvent): OutgoingMessage {
   134→    switch (event.type) {
   135→      case 'text':
   136→        return { type: 'text', text: event.content }
   137→      case 'thought':
   138→        return { type: 'thought', text: event.content }
   139→      case 'tool_call':
   140→        return { type: 'tool_call', text: event.name, metadata: { id: event.id, kind: event.kind, status: event.status, content: event.content, locations: event.locations } }
   141→      case 'tool_update':
   142→        return { type: 'tool_update', text: '', metadata: { id: event.id, status: event.status, content: event.content } }
   143→      case 'plan':
   144→        return { type: 'plan', text: '', metadata: { entries: event.entries } }
   145→      case 'usage':
   146→        return { type: 'usage', text: '', metadata: { tokensUsed: event.tokensUsed, contextSize: event.contextSize, cost: event.cost } }
   147→      default:
   148→        return { type: 'text', text: '' }
   149→    }
   150→  }
   151→
   152→  // Public — adapters call this for assistant session wiring
   153→  wireSessionEvents(session: Session, adapter: ChannelAdapter): void {
   154→    // Set adapter reference for autoName → renameSessionThread
   155→    session.adapter = adapter
   156→
   157→    session.agentInstance.onSessionUpdate = (event: AgentEvent) => {
   158→      switch (event.type) {
   159→        case 'text':
   160→        case 'thought':
   161→        case 'tool_call':
   162→        case 'tool_update':
   163→        case 'plan':
   164→        case 'usage':
   165→          adapter.sendMessage(session.id, this.toOutgoingMessage(event))
   166→          break
   167→
   168→        case 'session_end':
   169→          session.status = 'finished'
   170→          adapter.cleanupSkillCommands(session.id)
   171→          adapter.sendMessage(session.id, { type: 'session_end', text: `Done (${event.reason})` })
   172→          this.notificationManager.notify(session.channelId, {
   173→            sessionId: session.id,
   174→            sessionName: session.name,
   175→            type: 'completed',
   176→            summary: `Session "${session.name || session.id}" completed`,
   177→          })
   178→          break
   179→
   180→        case 'error':
   181→          adapter.cleanupSkillCommands(session.id)
   182→          adapter.sendMessage(session.id, { type: 'error', text: event.message })
   183→          this.notificationManager.notify(session.channelId, {
   184→            sessionId: session.id,
   185→            sessionName: session.name,
   186→            type: 'error',
   187→            summary: event.message,
   188→          })
   189→          break
   190→
   191→        case 'commands_update':
   192→          log.debug({ commands: event.commands }, 'Commands available')
   193→          adapter.sendSkillCommands(session.id, event.commands)
   194→          break
   195→      }
   196→    }
   197→
   198→    session.agentInstance.onPermissionRequest = async (request: PermissionRequest) => {
   199→      // Set pending BEFORE sending UI to avoid race condition
   200→      const promise = new Promise<string>((resolve) => {
   201→        session.pendingPermission = { requestId: request.id, resolve }
   202→      })
   203→
   204→      // Send permission UI to session topic (notification is sent by adapter)
   205→      await adapter.sendPermissionRequest(session.id, request)
   206→
   207→      // Wait for user response — adapter resolves this promise
   208→      return promise
   209→    }
   210→  }
   211→}

1→import * as schema from "./schema/index.js";
     2→export type * from "./schema/types.gen.js";
     3→export * from "./schema/index.js";
     4→export * from "./stream.js";
     5→import type { Stream } from "./stream.js";
     6→import type { Result, ErrorResponse, RequestHandler, NotificationHandler } from "./jsonrpc.js";
     7→/**
     8→ * An agent-side connection to a client.
     9→ *
    10→ * This class provides the agent's view of an ACP connection, allowing
    11→ * agents to communicate with clients. It implements the {@link Client} interface
    12→ * to provide methods for requesting permissions, accessing the file system,
    13→ * and sending session updates.
    14→ *
    15→ * See protocol docs: [Agent](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/overview#agent)
    16→ */
    17→export declare class AgentSideConnection {
    18→    #private;
    19→    /**
    20→     * Creates a new agent-side connection to a client.
    21→     *
    22→     * This establishes the communication channel from the agent's perspective
    23→     * following the ACP specification.
    24→     *
    25→     * @param toAgent - A function that creates an Agent handler to process incoming client requests
    26→     * @param stream - The bidirectional message stream for communication. Typically created using
    27→     *                 {@link ndJsonStream} for stdio-based connections.
    28→     *
    29→     * See protocol docs: [Communication Model](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/overview#communication-model)
    30→     */
    31→    constructor(toAgent: (conn: AgentSideConnection) => Agent, stream: Stream);
    32→    /**
    33→     * Handles session update notifications from the agent.
    34→     *
    35→     * This is a notification endpoint (no response expected) that sends
    36→     * real-time updates about session progress, including message chunks,
    37→     * tool calls, and execution plans.
    38→     *
    39→     * Note: Clients SHOULD continue accepting tool call updates even after
    40→     * sending a `session/cancel` notification, as the agent may send final
    41→     * updates before responding with the cancelled stop reason.
    42→     *
    43→     * See protocol docs: [Agent Reports Output](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/prompt-turn#3-agent-reports-output)
    44→     */
    45→    sessionUpdate(params: schema.SessionNotification): Promise<void>;
    46→    /**
    47→     * Requests permission from the user for a tool call operation.
    48→     *
    49→     * Called by the agent when it needs user authorization before executing
    50→     * a potentially sensitive operation. The client should present the options
    51→     * to the user and return their decision.
    52→     *
    53→     * If the client cancels the prompt turn via `session/cancel`, it MUST
    54→     * respond to this request with `RequestPermissionOutcome::Cancelled`.
    55→     *
    56→     * See protocol docs: [Requesting Permission](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/tool-calls#requesting-permission)
    57→     */
    58→    requestPermission(params: schema.RequestPermissionRequest): Promise<schema.RequestPermissionResponse>;
    59→    /**
    60→     * Reads content from a text file in the client's file system.
    61→     *
    62→     * Only available if the client advertises the `fs.readTextFile` capability.
    63→     * Allows the agent to access file contents within the client's environment.
    64→     *
    65→     * See protocol docs: [Client](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/overview#client)
    66→     */
    67→    readTextFile(params: schema.ReadTextFileRequest): Promise<schema.ReadTextFileResponse>;
    68→    /**
    69→     * Writes content to a text file in the client's file system.
    70→     *
    71→     * Only available if the client advertises the `fs.writeTextFile` capability.
    72→     * Allows the agent to create or modify files within the client's environment.
    73→     *
    74→     * See protocol docs: [Client](https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/overview#client)
    75→     */
    76→    writeTextFile(params: schema.WriteTextFileRequest): Promise<schema.WriteTextFileResponse>;
    77→    /**
    78→     * Executes a command in a new terminal.
    79→     *
    80→     * Returns a `TerminalHandle` that can be used to get output, wait for exit,
    81→     * kill the command, or release the terminal.
    82→     *
    83→     * The terminal can also be embedded in tool calls by using its ID in
    84→     * `ToolCallContent` with type "terminal".
    85→     *
    86→     * @param params - The terminal creation parameters
    87→     * @returns A handle to control and monitor the terminal
    88→     */
    89→    createTerminal(params: schema.CreateTerminalRequest): Promise<TerminalHandle>;
    90→    /**
    91→     * Extension method
    92→     *
    93→     * Allows the Agent to send an arbitrary request that is not part of the ACP spec.
    94→     */
    95→    extMethod(method: string, params: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<Record<string, unknown>>;
    96→    /**
    97→     * Extension notification
    98→     *
    99→     * Allows the Agent to send an arbitrary notification that is not part of the ACP spec.
   100→     */
   101→    extNotification(method: string, params: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<void>;
   102→    /**
   103→     * AbortSignal that aborts when the connection closes.
   104→     *
   105→     * This signal can be used to:
   106→     * - Listen for connection closure: `connection.signal.addEventListener('abort', () => {...})`
   107→     * - Check connection status synchronously: `if (connection.signal.aborted) {...}`
   108→     * - Pass to other APIs (fetch, setTimeout) for automatic cancellation
   109→     *
   110→     * The connection closes when the underlying stream ends, either normally or due to an error.
   111→     *
   112→     * @example
   113→     * ```typescript
   114→     * const connection = new AgentSideConnection(agent, stream);
   115→     *
   116→     * // Listen for closure
   117→     * connection.signal.addEventListener('abort', () => {
   118→     *   console.log('Connection closed - performing cleanup');
   119→     * });
   120→     *
   121→     * // Check status
   122→     * if (connection.signal.aborted) {
   123→     *   console.log('Connection is already closed');
   124→     * }
   125→     *
   126→     * // Pass to other APIs
   127→     * fetch(url, { signal: connection.signal });

     4→import type { SessionStatus } from './types.js'
     5→import { createChildLogger, createSessionLogger, type Logger } from './log.js'
     6→const moduleLog = createChildLogger({ module: 'session' })
     7→
     8→export class Session {
     9→  id: string
    10→  channelId: string
    11→  threadId: string = ''
    12→  agentName: string
    13→  workingDirectory: string
    14→  agentInstance: AgentInstance
    15→  status: SessionStatus = 'initializing'
    16→  name?: string

    19→  createdAt: Date = new Date()

    21→  pendingPermission?: { requestId: string; resolve: (optionId: string) => void }
    22→  log: Logger
    23→
    24→  constructor(opts: {
    25→    id?: string
    26→    channelId: string
    27→    agentName: string
    28→    workingDirectory: string
    29→    agentInstance: AgentInstance
    30→  }) {
    31→    this.id = opts.id || nanoid(12)
    32→    this.channelId = opts.channelId
    33→    this.agentName = opts.agentName
    34→    this.workingDirectory = opts.workingDirectory
    35→    this.agentInstance = opts.agentInstance
    36→    this.log = createSessionLogger(this.id, moduleLog)
    37→    this.log.info({ agentName: this.agentName }, 'Session created')
    38→  }
    39→
    40→  async enqueuePrompt(text: string): Promise<void> {
    41→    if (this.promptRunning) {
    42→      this.promptQueue.push(text)
    43→      this.log.debug({ queueDepth: this.promptQueue.length }, 'Prompt queued')
    44→      return
    45→    }
    46→    await this.runPrompt(text)
    47→  }
    48→
    49→  private async runPrompt(text: string): Promise<void> {
    50→    this.promptRunning = true
    51→    this.status = 'active'
    52→    const promptStart = Date.now()
    53→    this.log.debug('Prompt execution started')
    54→
    55→    try {

    58→
    59→      // Auto-name after first user prompt
    60→      if (!this.name) {
    61→        await this.autoName()
    62→      }
    63→    } catch (err) {
    64→      this.status = 'error'
    65→      this.log.error({ err }, 'Prompt execution failed')
    66→    } finally {
    67→      this.promptRunning = false
    68→
    69→      // Process next queued prompt
    70→      if (this.promptQueue.length > 0) {
    71→        const next = this.promptQueue.shift()!
    72→        await this.runPrompt(next)
    73→      }
    74→    }
    75→  }
    76→
    77→  // NOTE: This injects a summary prompt into the agent's conversation history.
    78→  // Known Phase 1 limitation — the agent sees this prompt in its context.
    79→  private async autoName(): Promise<void> {
    80→    let title = ''
    81→    const prevHandler = this.agentInstance.onSessionUpdate
    82→    this.agentInstance.onSessionUpdate = (event) => {
    83→      if (event.type === 'text') title += event.content
    84→    }
    85→
    86→    try {
    87→      await this.agentInstance.prompt(
    88→        'Summarize this conversation in max 5 words for a topic title. Reply ONLY with the title, nothing else.'
    89→      )
    90→      this.name = title.trim().slice(0, 50)
    91→      this.log.info({ name: this.name }, 'Session auto-named')

    93→      // Rename the topic on the channel

    97→    } catch {

Steps use checkbox (`- [ ]`) syntax for tracking. 4→ 5→**Goal:** Persist session-to-topic mappings so OpenACP can lazy-resume sessions after restart. 6→ 7→**Architecture:** A `SessionStore` in core persists `SessionRecord` entries (including platform-specific data like Telegram `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json`. On restart, when a user sends a message to an existing topic, the adapter triggers a lazy resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession()`, reconnect to topic. sessions: Record 239→} 240→ 241→const DEBOUNCE_MS = 2000 242→ 243→export class JsonFileSessionStore implements SessionStore { 244→ private records: Map = new Map() 245→ private filePath: string 246→ private ttlDays: number 247→ private debounceTimer: ReturnType | null = null 248→ private cleanupInterval: ReturnType | null = null 249→ 250→ constructor(filePath: string, ttlDays: number) { 251→ 1→# Session Persistence Design 2→ 3→## Problem 4→ 5→When OpenACP restarts, all session-to-topic mappings are lost. Telegram topics remain but OpenACP cannot reconnect sessions to them. Users must create new sessions even though `claude-agent-acp` stores conversation history on disk and supports session resume. 6→ 7→## Solution 8→ 9→Lazy resume with JSON file-based session store. 10→ 11→- Persist session metadata (including platform-specific data like `topicId`) to `~/.openacp/sessions.json` 12→- On restart, do NOT auto-resume any sessions (avoids spawning 100 subprocesses at once) 13→- When user sends a message to an existing topic, lazy-resume: spawn agent, call `unstable_resumeSession(agentSessionId)`, reconnect to topic 14→- If transcript is missing on disk, spawn a fresh session and update the record 15→- Auto-cleanup records older than 30 days (configurable) 16→ 17→## Data Model 18→ 19→### SessionRecord 20→ 21→```typescript 22→interface SessionRecord

> { 23→ sessionId: string // OpenACP session ID 24→ agentSessionId: string // ID from claude-agent-acp (used for resume) 25→ agentName: string 26→ workingDir: string 27→ channelId: string // "telegram" | "discord" | "slack" 28→ status: SessionStatus // reuse existing type: "initializing" | "active" | "cancelled" | "finished" | "error" 29→ createdAt: string // ISO timestamp (converted from Date on save) 30→ lastActiveAt: string // ISO timestamp, updated on each message 31→ name?: string // Auto-generated session name 32→ platform: P // Platform-specific data 33→} 34→``` 35→ 36→### Platform Metadata 37→ 38→Each adapter defines its own platform type: 39→ 40→```typescript 41→// Telegram 42→interface TelegramPlatformData { 43→ topicId: number 44→} 45→ 46→// Future: Discord 47→interface DiscordPlatformData { 48→ threadId: string 49→} 50→ 51→// Future: Slack 52→interface SlackPlatformData { 53→ threadTs: string 54→} 55→``` 56→ 57→### File Format 58→ 59→```json 60→{ 61→ "version": 1, 62→ "sessions": { ... } 63→} 64→``` 65→ 66→Version field enables future schema migrations. 67→ 68→## SessionStore Interface 69→ 70→```typescript 71→interface SessionStore { 72→ save(record: SessionRecord): Promise 73→ get(sessionId: string): SessionRecord | undefined 74→ findByPlatform(channelId: string, predicate: (platform: Record) => boolean): SessionRecord | undefined 75→ list(channelId?: string): SessionRecord[] 76→ remove(sessionId: string): Promise 77→} 78→``` 79→ 80→Note: `save()` and `remove()` are async for forward compatibility (SQLite swap). `get()`, `findByPlatform()`, `list()` are sync since they read from in-memory Map only. 81→ 82→`findByPlatform` uses a predicate instead of stringly-typed key/value for type safety: 83→```typescript 84→store.findByPlatform("telegram", (p) => p.topicId === 123) 85→``` 86→ 87→### JsonFileSessionStore 88→ 89→- Storage: `~/.openacp/sessions.json` 90→- In-memory `Map` is source of truth 91→- Debounced disk write (2s after last change) 92→- Force flush on SIGTERM/SIGINT 93→- Loads file once on startup 94→- Auto-cleanup on startup: remove records where `lastActiveAt` > TTL (default 30 days) 95→- Periodic cleanup: daily interval timer for long-running instances 96→ 97→## Lazy Resume Flow 98→ 99→``` 100→User message in topic 101→ → adapter receives message with topicId 102→ → store.findByPlatform("telegram", p => p.topicId === topicId) 103→ → Record found? 104→ → Yes: session active in memory? 105→ → Yes: forward message normally 106→ → No: acquire resume lock for topicId (prevent concurrent resume) 107→ → spawn agent subprocess 108→ → ACP handshake: connection.initialize() 109→ → call connection.unstable_resumeSession({ sessionId: agentSessionId, cwd: workingDir }) 110→ → if resume fails → fallback to connection.newSession({ cwd }) 111→ → wire events, attach to topic 112→ → release lock, forward message 113→ → No: spawn new session → create new record with topicId → forward 114→``` 115→ 116→### Concurrency Guard 117→ 118→A per-topic resume lock prevents duplicate spawns when multiple messages arrive for the same topic before resume completes. Messages arriving during resume are queued and forwarded after resume finishes. 119→ 120→### Resume Method Detail 121→ 122→`AgentInstance.resume()` follows the same flow as `spawn()`: 123→1. Resolve agent command 124→2. Spawn subprocess with stdio pipes 125→3. Create ACP connection 126→4. **`connection.initialize()`** — full ACP handshake (required for new subprocess) 127→5. **`connection.unstable_resumeSession({ sessionId, cwd })`** instead of `newSession()` 128→6. Set up crash detection and event wiring 129→7. If `unstable_resumeSession` fails → fallback to `newSession({ cwd })` 130→ 131→Note: `unstable_resumeSession` is marked `@experimental` in the ACP SDK. If removed in a future version, fallback to `loadSession()` (stable, replays history). 132→ 133→### Save Timing 134→ 135→`SessionRecord` is saved AFTER `AgentInstance.spawn()` completes, since `agentSessionId` is only available after ACP session creation. 136→ 137→## Record Lifecycle 138→ 139→### Writes 140→ 141→- `save()` on new session creation (after spawn completes and `agentSessionId` is available) 142→- `save()` on successful resume (update `agentSessionId` if it changed) 143→- Update `lastActiveAt` on each message (debounced 2s) 144→- Update `status` when session finishes, cancels, or errors 145→ 146→### Auto-cleanup 147→ 148→- Runs on startup + daily interval timer 149→- Removes records where `lastActiveAt` > `sessionStore.ttlDays` (default 30) 150→- Skips records with status `"active"` regardless of age 151→- Only removes records, does NOT delete Telegram topics 152→- TODO: Notify user about expired topics (not implemented) 153→ 154→### Debounce 155→ 156→- In-memory Map is source of truth 157→- Flush to disk 2s after last change 158→- Force flush on process shutdown 159→ 160→## Integration Points 161→ 162→### New File 163→ 164→- `src/core/session-store.ts` — `SessionStore` interface + `JsonFileSessionStore` 165→ 166→### Core Changes 167→ 168→- **SessionManager** — inject `SessionStore`, call `save()`/`remove()` on create/destroy 169→- **Session** — add `agentSessionId` field (from `AgentInstance.sessionId` after spawn completes) 170→ 171→### AgentInstance Changes 172→ 173→- Add static `resume()` method — same as `spawn()` but uses `unstable_resumeSession({ sessionId, cwd })` instead of `newSession()`, with fallback to `newSession()` on failure 174→ 175→### Telegram Adapter Changes 176→ 177→- Message handler: when receiving message for topic without active session → acquire resume lock → trigger lazy resume → release lock 178→- Queue messages arriving during resume, forward after completion 179→- Platform metadata type: `{ topicId: number }` 180→ 181→### Config Changes 182→ 183→- Add `sessionStore.ttlDays` to config schema (default 30) 184→ 185→## Edge Cases 186→ 187→- **Permission pending on restart**: Agent may re-request permission after resume. Existing permission handling in adapter will handle this naturally. 188→- **Cancelled sessions**: Records with status `"cancelled"` are not resumable. User message in a cancelled session's topic spawns a fresh session. 189→- **Long-running instances**: Daily cleanup timer prevents stale record accumulation without requiring restart. 190→ 191→## Design Decisions 192→ 193→1. **Lazy over eager resume** — Avoids spawning N subprocesses on startup. Only resume when user actually sends a message. 194→2. **JSON file over SQLite** — Simpler, no extra dependency. SessionStore interface allows swapping to SQLite later if needed. Version field in file format enables future migrations. 195→3. **Core-level store, adapter-level platform data** — Adapters share storage logic via `SessionStore`, only define their own `platform` fields. Adding a new adapter = define platform type + use existing store API. 196→4. **Auto-cleanup records, not topics** — Records are internal data, safe to auto-delete. Topics are user-visible, should not be auto-deleted. 197→5. **Predicate-based lookup** — `findByPlatform` uses predicate for type safety instead of stringly-typed key/value. 198→6. **Resume lock** — Per-topic lock prevents race condition from concurrent messages during lazy resume. 199→

1→import { ConfigManager } from './config.js'
     2→import { AgentManager } from './agent-manager.js'
     3→import { SessionManager } from './session-manager.js'
     4→import { NotificationManager } from './notification.js'
     5→import { ChannelAdapter } from './channel.js'
     6→import { Session } from './session.js'
     7→import type { IncomingMessage, AgentEvent, OutgoingMessage, PermissionRequest } from './types.js'
     8→import { createChildLogger } from './log.js'
     9→const log = createChildLogger({ module: 'core' })
    10→
    11→export class OpenACPCore {
    12→  configManager: ConfigManager
    13→  agentManager: AgentManager
    14→  sessionManager: SessionManager
    15→  notificationManager: NotificationManager
    16→  adapters: Map<string, ChannelAdapter> = new Map()
    17→
    18→  constructor(configManager: ConfigManager) {
    19→    this.configManager = configManager
    20→    const config = configManager.get()
    21→    this.agentManager = new AgentManager(config)
    22→    this.sessionManager = new SessionManager()
    23→    this.notificationManager = new NotificationManager(this.adapters)
    24→  }
    25→
    26→  registerAdapter(name: string, adapter: ChannelAdapter): void {
    27→    this.adapters.set(name, adapter)
    28→  }
    29→
    30→  async start(): Promise<void> {
    31→    for (const adapter of this.adapters.values()) {
    32→      await adapter.start()
    33→    }
    34→  }
    35→
    36→  async stop(): Promise<void> {
    37→    // 1. Notify users
    38→    try {
    39→      await this.notificationManager.notifyAll({
    40→        sessionId: 'system',
    41→        type: 'error',
    42→        summary: 'OpenACP is shutting down',
    43→      })
    44→    } catch { /* best effort */ }
    45→
    46→    // 2. Destroy all sessions
    47→    await this.sessionManager.destroyAll()
    48→
    49→    // 3. Stop adapters
    50→    for (const adapter of this.adapters.values()) {
    51→      await adapter.stop()
    52→    }
    53→  }
    54→
    55→  // --- Message Routing ---
    56→
    57→  async handleMessage(message: IncomingMessage): Promise<void> {
    58→    const config = this.configManager.get()
    59→    log.debug({ channelId: message.channelId, threadId: message.threadId, userId: message.userId }, 'Incoming message')
    60→
    61→    // Security: check allowed user IDs
    62→    if (config.security.allowedUserIds.length > 0) {
    63→      if (!config.security.allowedUserIds.includes(message.userId)) {
    64→        log.warn({ userId: message.userId }, 'Rejected message from unauthorized user')
    65→        return
    66→      }
    67→    }
    68→
    69→    // Check concurrent session limit
    70→    const activeSessions = this.sessionManager.listSessions()
    71→      .filter(s => s.status === 'active' || s.status === 'initializing')
    72→    if (activeSessions.length >= config.security.maxConcurrentSessions) {
    73→      log.warn({ userId: message.userId, currentCount: activeSessions.length, max: config.security.maxConcurrentSessions }, 'Session limit reached')
    74→      const adapter = this.adapters.get(message.channelId)
    75→      if (adapter) {
    76→        await adapter.sendMessage('system', {
    77→          type: 'error',
    78→          text: `Max concurrent sessions (${config.security.maxConcurrentSessions}) reached. Cancel a session first.`,
    79→        })
    80→      }
    81→      return
    82→    }
    83→
    84→    // Find session by thread
    85→    const session = this.sessionManager.getSessionByThread(message.channelId, message.threadId)
    86→    if (!session) return
    87→
    88→    // Forward to session
    89→    await session.enqueuePrompt(message.text)
    90→  }
    91→
    92→  async handleNewSession(
    93→    channelId: string,
    94→    agentName?: string,
    95→    workspacePath?: string,
    96→  ): Promise<Session> {
    97→    const config = this.configManager.get()
    98→    const resolvedAgent = agentName || config.defaultAgent
    99→    log.info({ channelId, agentName: resolvedAgent }, 'New session request')
   100→    const resolvedWorkspace = this.configManager.resolveWorkspace(
   101→      workspacePath || config.agents[resolvedAgent]?.workingDirectory
   102→    )
   103→
   104→    const session = await this.sessionManager.createSession(
   105→      channelId, resolvedAgent, resolvedWorkspace, this.agentManager
   106→    )
   107→
   108→    // Wire events
   109→    const adapter = this.adapters.get(channelId)
   110→    if (adapter) {
   111→      this.wireSessionEvents(session, adapter)
   112→    }
   113→
   114→    return session
   115→  }
   116→
   117→  async handleNewChat(
   118→    channelId: string,
   119→    currentThreadId: string,
   120→  ): Promise<Session | null> {
   121→    const currentSession = this.sessionManager.getSessionByThread(channelId, currentThreadId)
   122→    if (!currentSession) return null
   123→
   124→    return this.handleNewSession(
   125→      channelId,
   126→      currentSession.agentName,
   127→      currentSession.workingDirectory,
   128→    )
   129→  }
   130→
   131→  // --- Event Wiring ---
   132→
   133→  private toOutgoingMessage(event: AgentEvent): OutgoingMessage {
   134→    switch (event.type) {
   135→      case 'text':
   136→        return { type: 'text', text: event.content }
   137→      case 'thought':
   138→        return { type: 'thought', text: event.content }
   139→      case 'tool_call':
   140→        return { type: 'tool_call', text: event.name, metadata: { id: event.id, kind: event.kind, status: event.status, content: event.content, locations: event.locations } }
   141→      case 'tool_update':
   142→        return { type: 'tool_update', text: '', metadata: { id: event.id, status: event.status, content: event.content } }
   143→      case 'plan':
   144→        return { type: 'plan', text: '', metadata: { entries: event.entries } }
   145→      case 'usage':
   146→        return { type: 'usage', text: '', metadata: { tokensUsed: event.tokensUsed, contextSize: event.contextSize, cost: event.cost } }
   147→      default:
   148→        return { type: 'text', text: '' }
   149→    }
   150→  }
   151→
   152→  // Public — adapters call this for assistant session wiring
   153→  wireSessionEvents(session: Session, adapter: ChannelAdapter): void {
   154→    // Set adapter reference for autoName → renameSessionThread
   155→    session.adapter = adapter
   156→
   157→    session.agentInstance.onSessionUpdate = (event: AgentEvent) => {
   158→      switch (event.type) {
   159→        case 'text':
   160→        case 'thought':
   161→        case 'tool_call':
   162→        case 'tool_update':
   163→        case 'plan':
   164→        case 'usage':
   165→          adapter.sendMessage(session.id, this.toOutgoingMessage(event))
   166→          break
   167→
   168→        case 'session_end':
   169→          session.status = 'finished'
   170→          adapter.cleanupSkillCommands(session.id)
   171→          adapter.sendMessage(session.id, { type: 'session_end', text: `Done (${event.reason})` })
   172→          this.notificationManager.notify(session.channelId, {
   173→            sessionId: session.id,
   174→            sessionName: session.name,
   175→            type: 'completed',
   176→            summary: `Session "${session.name || session.id}" completed`,
   177→          })
   178→          break
   179→
   180→        case 'error':
   181→          adapter.cleanupSkillCommands(session.id)
   182→          adapter.sendMessage(session.id, { type: 'error', text: event.message })
   183→          this.notificationManager.notify(session.channelId, {
   184→            sessionId: session.id,
   185→            sessionName: session.name,
   186→            type: 'error',
   187→            summary: event.message,
   188→          })
   189→          break
   190→
   191→        case 'commands_update':
   192→          log.debug({ commands: event.commands }, 'Commands available')
   193→          adapter.sendSkillCommands(session.id, event.commands)
   194→          break
   195→      }
   196→    }
   197→
   198→    session.agentInstance.onPermissionRequest = async (request: PermissionRequest) => {
   199→      // Set pending BEFORE sending UI to avoid race condition
   200→      const promise = new Promise<string>((resolve) => {
   201→        session.pendingPermission = { requestId: request.id, resolve }
   202→      })
   203→
   204→      // Send permission UI to session topic (notification is sent by adapter)
   205→      await adapter.sendPermissionRequest(session.id, request)
   206→
   207→      // Wait for user response — adapter resolves this promise
   208→      return promise
   209→    }
   210→  }
   211→}
